title: The Corner NR PLUS Trade Breaking News: Americans Pay the Tariffs Their Government Imposes on Them By John R
raw_response: {"rating": 1.9, "lean": "Lean Right", "explanation": "Opinion/editorial. The author advances a consistent free‑market critique of tariffs, portraying them as consumer‑paid taxes and government coercion, which aligns with right‑leaning/libertarian economics. Despite criticizing a Republican figure, the policy framing is pro–free trade and anti‑intervention, warranting a Lean Right rating of +1.9. Detailed Analysis: (1) Frames tariffs as taxes that coercively redirect household money to the government, a limited‑government, anti‑tax stance; (2) Emphasizes that voluntary market transactions benefit both sides and that blocking them harms welfare, a classic free‑market argument; (3) Labels the idea of tariff “free lunch” as a farce and characterizes proposed hikes as punishing Americans by raising taxes on imports, reinforcing anti‑protectionist, anti‑intervention views; (4) Invokes Adam Smith as authoritative validation of market‑liberal principles; (5) Selects supportive economic studies and reporting while omitting protectionist rationales (e.g., domestic industry or national‑security arguments), indicating one‑sided pro–free trade framing."}

parsed_output.explanation: Opinion/editorial. The author advances a consistent free‑market critique of tariffs, portraying them as consumer‑paid taxes and government coercion, which aligns with right‑leaning/libertarian economics. Despite criticizing a Republican figure, the policy framing is pro–free trade and anti‑intervention, warranting a Lean Right rating of +1.9. Detailed Analysis: (1) Frames tariffs as taxes that coercively redirect household money to the government, a limited‑government, anti‑tax stance; (2) Emphasizes that voluntary market transactions benefit both sides and that blocking them harms welfare, a classic free‑market argument; (3) Labels the idea of tariff “free lunch” as a farce and characterizes proposed hikes as punishing Americans by raising taxes on imports, reinforcing anti‑protectionist, anti‑intervention views; (4) Invokes Adam Smith as authoritative validation of market‑liberal principles; (5) Selects supportive economic studies and reporting while omitting protectionist rationales (e.g., domestic industry or national‑security arguments), indicating one‑sided pro–free trade framing.
